ANDREW: Then every market and every diplomatic relationship in the region moves fast. The Pentagon has put the conflict's total cost at roughly twenty-five billion dollars so far, according to officials briefed on the military budget. We are watching this by the hour.

ANDREW: On the Voting Rights Act — the ruling came last Wednesday, six to three along ideological lines. The case was Louisiana versus Callais. At issue was Louisiana's redrawn congressional map, which had created a second majority-Black district. Justice Alito wrote the majority opinion, finding that using racial data as the predominant motivation for drawing those lines violates the Equal Protection Clause. The ruling weakens Section Two of the Voting Rights Act — the provision that has been used for decades to protect minority voters from being diluted out of political power.

JENNY: Walk me through the contradiction there — doesn't Section Two sometimes actually require majority-minority districts?

ANDREW: That is exactly the legal tension at the center of this. What the Court said, effectively, is that even VRA compliance cannot justify using race as the predominant factor when drawing a district. The ACLU and the Brennan Center are calling it the most consequential VRA ruling since Shelby County in 2013, which gutted the preclearance requirement. The practical fallout was swift — Louisiana suspended its May 16th primary the next day to allow the legislature time to redraw the map. Republicans in Alabama, Georgia, and South Carolina are already examining whether their own redistricting can benefit from the ruling ahead of November's midterms. This decision will shape the composition of Congress for the next cycle at minimum.

ANDREW: Briefly on GDP — the Bureau of Economic Analysis confirmed first-quarter growth at two percent annualized. That came in just below the consensus forecast of two-point-two percent, but it is a substantial improvement from the fourth quarter's revised half-percent. Investment and consumer spending drove the gain. Imports weighed on the headline as businesses frontloaded purchases ahead of anticipated tariff changes. I will connect the GDP number to what it means for the rate outlook in a moment.

JENNY: Andrew, let me take it from here on the tech side, because the biggest AI story of this weekend comes with a real irony at its center.

JENNY: On Friday, the Pentagon finalized classified artificial intelligence contracts with at least seven major companies — OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Oracle, Nvidia, and SpaceX. These deals expand AI access into sensitive Defense Department environments: intelligence analysis, logistics, large-scale classified data processing. The company that did not get a deal was Anthropic. The Pentagon has maintained a formal supply-chain risk designation on Anthropic since March — a label historically reserved for companies tied to foreign adversaries, and the first time any American company has received it.

ANDREW: What is the actual sticking point?

JENNY: The contract language. The Defense Department wanted Anthropic to permit its Claude model to be used for, quote, all lawful purposes. Anthropic refused, arguing that language could authorize domestic mass surveillance and fully autonomous weapons systems. Anthropic sued the administration. Courts have so far declined to block the Pentagon's designation, and the contracts with the other companies went out Friday anyway. Here is where it gets interesting: the same week Anthropic is being frozen out of government work, it is having a remarkable commercial run. Secondary market trading platforms are now pricing Anthropic above one trillion dollars in valuation — putting it ahead of OpenAI for the first time. And TechCrunch reported late last week that a formal funding round at over nine hundred billion dollars could close within two weeks.

ANDREW: So the company the Pentagon blacklisted might be the world's most valuable AI company by end of the month.

JENNY: That is the situation we are in. There is also a White House angle here — Trump met with Anthropic's CEO Dario Amodei in April and called a deal, quote, possible. Chief of staff Susie Wiles was in that meeting. But the Pentagon moved before any executive-level resolution materialized. Whether the White House steps in to restore Anthropic's access to federal contracts is one of the bigger unresolved policy questions in AI right now.

JENNY: One more note from the tech world — a preprint circulating among emergency medicine researchers found that OpenAI's o1 model correctly identified the diagnosis for roughly 67 percent of emergency room patients when given electronic records and a brief nurse intake summary. Triage physicians in the same comparison were accurate in about 50 to 55 percent of cases. The paper is not yet peer reviewed, but multiple major health systems are already running parallel AI-assisted triage pilots. It is a signal that clinical AI has moved well past the proof-of-concept stage.

ANDREW: Good context, Jenny. Let me turn to the mortgage market, because rates had a telling week and the story behind the move connects to everything we have been talking about.

ANDREW: Freddie Mac's Thursday survey put the thirty-year fixed at six-point-three percent — up from six-point-two-three the previous week. That breaks three consecutive weeks of improvement, which had been the best spring rate window in three homebuying seasons. The uptick came from oil-driven pressure on the ten-year Treasury. The mechanism is direct: when Hormuz tensions rise, inflation expectations move, bond prices fall, yields rise, and mortgage rates follow within days. The Hormuz situation is not an abstraction for home buyers right now — it is showing up in their rate sheet.

JENNY: Does a seven-basis-point move actually change buyer behavior at the margin?

ANDREW: It takes some urgency off the table. The Mortgage Bankers Association reported that applications for the week ending April 24th were down about one-and-a-half percent. More revealing is the composition — adjustable-rate mortgages climbed to eight-point-three percent of total applications. That is buyers betting rates will fall later rather than locking a fixed rate now. They are hedging, which tells you the psychology is fragile. If Project Freedom succeeds and oil retreats, you could see the thirty-year pull back toward the low sixes relatively quickly. If Hormuz stays contested, that rate goes in the other direction.

ANDREW: The other factor shaping the rate outlook is the Fed transition. Kevin Warsh's nomination cleared the Senate Banking Committee thirteen to eleven on April 29th — every Republican for, every Democrat against, the most partisan Fed chair committee vote on record. The full Senate floor vote is expected the week of May 11th. Jerome Powell's term expires May 15th. Warsh will walk into the chair role on the other side of a live military operation, a spring housing market under oil pressure, and a GDP print that keeps the economy in a hold-steady zone. His first public statements on rate direction after confirmation will move markets. Lenders are already recommending buyers lock in now rather than wait to see what he signals.

JENNY: So buyers who have been waiting for rates to fall further may be waiting a bit longer than they hoped.

ANDREW: That is the responsible framing. And sellers who were thinking of holding inventory until rates dropped further may want to reconsider the timing.

JENNY: Let me bring you to Jacksonville. Weather-wise, it is a beautiful start to the week on the First Coast — 86 degrees today, low of 70 overnight, clear skies. A welcome reset after rain moved through late last week.

JENNY: The forward-looking story worth flagging is a sign of where downtown Jacksonville is heading. Whole Foods Market is opening a thirty-eight-thousand-square-foot store at 1 Riverside Avenue in the Brooklyn neighborhood on May 21st. Downtown grocery access has been a persistent sticking point in the city's urban core redevelopment push for years — cited in almost every conversation about whether downtown can actually attract residents, not just visitors. A premium national grocer committing to that corridor is a signal that foot traffic on Riverside Avenue has reached a real threshold. The store's design nods to the Florida Times-Union building that once stood on the site, and the product assortment features more than 800 local Florida items.

ANDREW: What is the broader context for that timing?

JENNY: It is almost too well timed. The Jacksonville Jazz Festival runs the same weekend — May 21st through May 24th, Memorial Day weekend — drawing tens of thousands of people into the core. The Jaguars stadium reconstruction is at fifteen percent complete, on track for an August 2028 finish. JTA investments in the corridor continue. When you add a national grocery anchor to that picture, you start to see downtown Jacksonville making a genuine argument for itself as a place to live. The Whole Foods opening is a data point in that argument.

JENNY: On a harder note for local wallets — Duval County gas prices hit four dollars and thirty cents a gallon last week, matching the four-year national high tied directly to the Hormuz disruption. Before the Iran conflict began in late February, the local average was around three dollars and fourteen cents. That is more than a dollar-per-gallon increase in about ten weeks, paid by every commuter, every delivery driver, and every small business running a fleet in Northeast Florida. Every tanker that gets through the strait today is a data point on when that number starts coming down.

JENNY: Two more quick items from the First Coast. Duval County's school bus stop-arm camera program is now issuing real fines — two hundred twenty-five dollars per violation. The warning period ended last week. Blanding Boulevard has been flagged as the highest-violation corridor in the county. If you drive that stretch during school hours, treat every school bus stop as a camera event, because it very likely is one. And updating a story we have been tracking — the Railroad Fire in Clay and Putnam counties was at about 80 percent containment as of late last week, and the rain that moved through Thursday and Friday gave crews the moisture they had been waiting on for weeks. Full containment appears close.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today — and genuinely just today: the first convoy attempt through the Strait of Hormuz. Project Freedom is live. Fifteen thousand service members are in those waters right now. Iran has threatened to fire. The strait has been operating at a fraction of its normal capacity since the war began in February. If the first convoy gets through without incident, watch for an oil price pullback by end of day — which translates directly into the ten-year Treasury, into mortgage rates, and into what you pay at the pump here in Jacksonville. If there is an incident, you are looking at a fundamentally different week. Check U.S. Central Command's public channels and any major wire service for convoy updates this afternoon. This is as live as a story gets on a Monday morning.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Monday. Have a great start to the week.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.
